testCases.title: Verify that the /api/deserialize endpoint rejects gadget-chain deserialisation payloads (ObjectInputFilter whitelisting)
testCases.owasp: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
testCases.description: The /api/deserialize endpoint previously used unsafe native Java deserialisation (ObjectInputStream.readObject) without a filter, which allowed attacker-controlled classes to be instantiated on the server (gadget-chain RCE). The fix installs an ObjectInputFilter that whitelists only com.owasp.lab.model.*, java.lang.* primitives/wrappers and a small set of java.util.* collection types, and rejects every other class before readObject() is called. This test confirms the filter is active and blocks non-whitelisted classes.
preconditions: Application is running and the /api/deserialize endpoint is reachable.
A base64-encoded Java serialised payload can be generated (e.g. using ysoserial or a small Java helper).
The reviewer has access to a list of known gadget-chain classes (e.g. org.apache.commons.collections.functors.InvokerTransformer, org.springframework.beans.factory.ObjectFactory).
A legitimate com.owasp.lab.model.* DTO class is available for the positive-path case.
testSteps: Step 1: Generate a Java serialised object for a whitelisted class (com.owasp.lab.model.*) using a benign DTO, then Base64-encode it.
Step 2: Send a POST request to /api/deserialize with the Base64 payload in the request body (Content-Type: application/octet-stream or text/plain depending on the controller binding).
Step 3: Confirm the server responds with HTTP 200 and the deserialised object is processed (positive baseline).
Step 4: Generate a Java serialised object for a non-whitelisted gadget class (e.g. org.apache.commons.collections.functors.InvokerTransformer) using ysoserial, then Base64-encode it.
Step 5: Send a POST request to /api/deserialize with the gadget payload.
Step 6: Observe the response: it MUST be rejected (HTTP 400, 422 or 500 with an InvalidClassException) and MUST NOT execute any constructor or static initialiser of the gadget class.
Step 7: Repeat steps 4-6 with at least one additional non-whitelisted class (e.g. a spring-core or groovy gadget) to confirm the filter is class-agnostic.
Step 8: Verify the server logs do NOT contain evidence of a ClassNotFoundException bypass or filter bypass.
testCases.expectedResult: The whitelisted DTO payload is accepted and processed normally. Every non-whitelisted (gadget) class payload is rejected by the ObjectInputFilter before readObject() invokes its constructor. No remote code execution occurs. The response for a blocked payload is an error status (e.g. 400/422/500) and never a successful deserialisation of a non-whitelisted class.
references: OWASP Top 10 2021 - A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures

testCases.title: Verify that /api/comment/greet HTML-escapes the 'name' query parameter to prevent reflected XSS
testCases.description: The /api/comment/greet endpoint previously concatenated the 'name' query parameter directly into an HTML response, allowing a reflected XSS payload such as <script>alert('XSS')</script> to execute in the victim's browser. The fix HTML-escapes the five special characters (&, <, >, ", ') before interpolating the parameter, so any HTML/JS payload is rendered as inert text. This test verifies that the escaping is applied to the reflected response.
preconditions: Application is running and the /api/comment/greet endpoint is reachable.
A browser or HTTP client capable of rendering the response as text/html.
Access to URL-encoding utilities for constructing the test payloads.
testSteps: Step 1: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name=Alice and confirm the response body contains 'Hello, Alice!' (positive baseline).

Step 3: Inspect the raw response body and confirm the literal characters '<script>alert('XSS')</script>' (or equivalent entity-encoded form) are present and that the raw characters '<script>' and '</script>' do NOT appear in the response.
Step 4: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name= and confirm the response contains '<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>' (or equivalent entity-encoded form) and the raw '

Step 5: Send GET /api/comment/greet?name="><svg/onload=alert(1)> and confirm the response contains the entity-encoded form ('"><svg/onload=alert(1)>' or equivalent) and the raw characters '"' followed by '>' are not present un-escaped.
Step 6: Render the response in a real browser and confirm no JavaScript alert/dialog fires.
Step 7: Send GET /api/comment/greet with no name parameter and confirm the response defaults to 'Hello, World!' (regression check for null handling).
Step 8: Check the response Content-Type header is text/html and not reflected into an executable context (e.g. not served as text/javascript).
testCases.expectedResult: The legitimate 'Hello, Alice!' behaviour is preserved. All HTML/JS metacharacters in the 'name' parameter are entity-encoded in the response, so the browser renders them as text and does not execute any injected script, image onerror, or SVG onload handler. No alert dialog fires when the page is rendered.
references: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
